identity.research_question: The aim is to create a novel 3D HGSC model through high-throughput drug testing.
identity.project_summary: This project aims to elucidate how cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) in high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) influence responses to emerging anti-cancer therapies. To achieve this, we will develop a novel 3D ex vivo model integrating patient-derived HGSC tumor cells, tumor-resident immune cells, and CAFs to more accurately recapitulate the complexity of the tumor microenvironment. Using this model, we will then perform functional drug-response assays to identify CAF-mediated mechanisms of therapeutic resistance and uncover potential strategies to improve treatment efficacy.

protocols.title: ## Sheet: Dissociation
steps: Minced tissue method provides fairly homogenous fibroblastic cell-cultures.
Therefore, this method assures the isolation of a variety of different subpopulations of fibroblasts present in a given (normal or tumor-associated) tissue.
The second method, enzymatic digestion method (Section 3.1.2), is faster and yields a higher recovery rate of cells from the tissue.
"Transfer the material into a sterile 150 ml Erlenmeyer.
Add 8 ml DMEM-3%BSAPen/Strep into the 10-cm plastic dish and collect the remaining tissue pieces transferring them into the same 150 ml Erlenmeyer.
Add 2 ml of Collagenase-3 (10X) the samples thus diluting the collagenase 10 fold.
After 1h dissociation, if undigested tumor pieces still remain continue the agitation until complete digestion is evident.
Fresh collagenase-3 can be added or, alternatively, the tissue pieces can be mechanically dissociated by carefully pipetting up and down.
Louis, MO, USA) and mixed with 1% collagenase and 1% hyaluronidase (Invitrogen).
The enzymatically disaggregated cell suspensions were filtered (70-μm cell strainer) and washed twice with PBS.
All the cells were separated on a gradient of Percoll Plus (the density of the top band and bottom layer was 45 and 90%, respectively) (GE Healthcare).
digested by collagenase type I, collagenase type III and hyaluronidase (1.5 mg/ml, Sigma Aldrich) in DMEM with 10% FBS.

document_index.excerpt: DMEM/F12+++ = DMEM/F12 supplemented with 1X glutamax, 10 mM HEPES, and Fibroblast media = DMEM/F12+++ +10 % FBS Small tissue pieces were placed in a 6-well plate. Approx. 6 pieces per well and with 800 ul media per well. No digestion was used. 300 ul medium was added on day 1. The tissue pieces were removed from the wells as enough fibroblasts had isolated and attached to the plate. Wells were washed once with PDB and 2 ml fresh media was added. Media was changed every Mon, Wed, and Fri. Before the media change, wells were washed once with PBS. Wells 1 and 2 were trypsinized (0.25% trypsin EDTA, 3 min at +37C) and pooled together for plating. document_index.excerpt: Spatially-derived fibroblast signatures If TSI score Stroma score → assign "TSI-like” Else → assign "Stroma-like” I used (thanks to Anna V.): rescale01 <- function(x) { (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x)) to rescale, so the lowest score becomes 0, the highest becomes 1, and all other values are proportionally scaled in between. Calculation for Normalized Enrichment Score (NES) Average log2 fold change of each gene between TSI_high and Stroma_high is calculated Genes are ranked by how much they are higher in TSI_high versus Stroma_high GSEA checks whether genes from each pathway tend to appear near the top or bottom of this list A running sum over the ranked genes gives the raw enrichment score (ES) Random permutations: To know whether the observed clustering is unusual, GSEA shuffles the rank

document_index.excerpt: This document includes instructions on how to prepare stock solutions for icPDC media, OmGel, dissociation enzymes, and dead cell marker (DCM) # Stock solutions for icPDC media To reconstitute certain reagents, a 0.1% BSA solution is required. Note that the amount of 15 mg is based on the limitations of the available scale, which cannot accurately weigh less than 10 mg. Dissolve 15 mg in 15 mL DPBS (without Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺); the orbital shaker in the cold room can be used to aid dissolution. Sterile filter with a 0.22 µm strainer before use. document_index.name: README.md
document_index.title: Project introduction: This project aims to elucidate how
document_index.excerpt: Project introduction: This project aims to elucidate how cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) in high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) influence responses to emerging anti-cancer therapies. To achieve this, we will develop a novel 3D ex vivo model integrating patient-derived HGSC tumor cells, tumor-resident immune cells, and CAFs to more accurately recapitulate the complexity of the tumor microenvironment. Using this model, we will then perform functional drug-response assays to identify CAF-mediated mechanisms of therapeutic resistance and uncover potential strategies to improve treatment Project lead: María Hincapié-Otero, MSc (Doctoral researcher, 2023 - Project members: María Hincapié-Otero, Aino Elomaa, Sarah Wolf, Nika Mikhailava, Kürsat Birgin, Sara Palomino, Foteini Chamchougia E

## Page 1
Cancer -associated fibroblasts condition the ovarian cancer microenvironment and 
therapeutic responses to antibody -drug conjugates and immunotherapy 
 
María Hincapié -Otero1*, Nika Mikhailava¹, Kürşat Birgin¹, Aino Elomaa1,2, Sarah Wolf¹, Sara Palomino1,3, Iga 
Niemiec¹, Zhihan Liang¹, Aleksandra Shabanova¹, Joonas Jukonen1, Bassel Alsaed4, Tuula Salo5, Anni Virtanen1,6, 
Ulla-Maija Haltia1,7, Heidi Haikala4,8, Anniina Färkkilä1,3,7 
 
1 Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Finland 
2 Institute of Biomedicine, Faculty of Medicine, University of Turku, Finland 
3 Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute of Life Sciences, University of Helsinki, Finland 
4 Research Program in Translational Immunology, University of Helsinki, Finland 
5 Department of Oral and Maxillofacial Diseases, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland 
6 Department of Pathology, University of Helsinki and HUS Diagnostic Center, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland 
7 Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Helsinki University Hospital, Finland 
8 Faculty of Medicine and Health Technology, Tampere University, Finland 
 
 
Correspondence should be addressed to A.F. ( anniina.farkkila@helsinki.fi ) 
Introduction 
Cancer -associated fibroblasts (CAFs) are key regulators of the tumor microenvironment (TME), but how 
they drive immune evasion and shape responses to emerging therapies remains unclear. We combined 
spatial and single -cell transcriptomics with functional ex -vivo assays to define therapeutically relevant CAF 
phenotypes and assess their impact on anti -TROP2 antibody -drug conjugates (ADCs) and immunotherapy 
responses. 
Methodology

combined 
spatial and single -cell transcriptomics with functional ex -vivo assays to define therapeutically relevant CAF 
phenotypes and assess their impact on anti -TROP2 antibody -drug conjugates (ADCs) and immunotherapy 
responses. 
Methodology 
We employed GeoMX spatial transcriptomics ( N = 40, AOIs = 705 ) of FFPE HGSC tumors to define spatially 
distinct fibroblast programs. We generated a tumor –stroma interface (TSI) –associated signature and 
projected it onto scRNA -seq data from HGSC tumors ( N = 29 ) to refine CAF states. We then scored each 
fibroblast based on signature expression and annotated them as TSI -like or stroma -like and performed 
pathway analyses in both datasets. We tested ADCs and immunotherapies in patient -derived 3D HGSC 
TME models +/ - CAFs to evaluate how CAF states modulate responses using scRNA -seq and flow 
cytometry. 
Results 
Spatial transcriptomics revealed a distinct TGFβ -driven CAF program enriched for extracellular matrix 
organization, cell adhesion, and tissue remodeling pathways at the TSI. Projection of the spatial gene 
signature onto scRNA -seq data revealed that the TSI -CAFs expressed predominantly myCAF phenotypes. 
Spatial immune deconvolution showed that TSI -CAFs associated with significantly decreased infiltration of 
NK cells, B cells, CD8 ⁺ and CD4 ⁺ T cells suggesting an immune -excluded interface driven by CAFs. This 
spatial immune -exclusion was also associated with higher expression of TROP2 in cancer cells, providing 
a rationale for TROP2 -targeted ADC -immunotherapy combination strategies. To functionally assess how 
TSI-CAFs modulate therapeutic responses to these age nts ex -vivo, we established and validated a modular 

## Page 2

in cancer cells, providing 
a rationale for TROP2 -targeted ADC -immunotherapy combination strategies. To functionally assess how 
TSI-CAFs modulate therapeutic responses to these age nts ex -vivo, we established and validated a modular 

## Page 2
3D HGSC TME model enabling controlled inclusion or exclusion of CAFs, allowing evaluation of responses 
to anti -TROP2 ADCs and immunotherapies. 
Conclusion 
myCAFs create an immune -suppressed TSI associated with higher ADC target expression. This highlights 
the importance of combining ADCs with immunotherapies to re -activate HGSC anti -tumor immunity.

**Spatial Transcriptomics Reveals Spatially Distinct Fibroblast Gene
Signatures in High-Grade Serous Carcinoma**

Nika Mikhailava¹, María Hincapié-Otero¹, Joonas Jukonen¹, Iga Niemiec¹,
Zhihan Liang¹, Aleksandra Shabanova¹, Matilda Salko¹, Ulla-Maija
Haltia¹², Anni Virtanen¹³, Anniina Färkkilä¹²³⁴

¹ Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki,
Helsinki, Finland\
² Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Department of Oncology,
Clinical Trials Unit, Comprehensive Cancer Center, Helsinki University
Hospital, Helsinki, Finland\
³ Department of Pathology, University of Helsinki and HUS Diagnostic
Center, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland\
⁴ Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute for Life
Sciences, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland

**Introduction**

High-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most common subtype of ovarian
cancer and the deadliest gynecological malignancy. Although most
patients initially respond to platinum-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy
(NACT), resistance typically develops, with relapse rates of 70--80%.
The tumor microenvironment (TME), particularly cancer-associated
fibroblasts (CAFs), contributes to resistance. Targeting CAF-derived
pathways offers a novel potential strategy for platinum-resistant
patients.

**Methods**

Formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) post-NACT tumor samples (n =
40) from HGSC patients enrolled in the Oncosys-OVA clinical trial
(NCTXXYY) were profiled using GeoMx spatial transcriptomics.
Fibroblast-specific gene expression was deconvoluted using the
BayesPrism algorithm.

**Results**

A total of 611 stroma-localized Areas of Illumination (AOIs) were
analyzed (tumor--stroma interface \[TSI\] = 309, distant stroma = 302).

scriptomics.
Fibroblast-specific gene expression was deconvoluted using the
BayesPrism algorithm.

**Results**

A total of 611 stroma-localized Areas of Illumination (AOIs) were
analyzed (tumor--stroma interface \[TSI\] = 309, distant stroma = 302).
Hierarchical clustering of the most variable genes revealed candidate
signatures distinguishing TSI from distant stroma. CAFs in the two
compartments displayed distinct transcriptional profiles, with the most
variable genes showing higher expression in distant stroma. These
distant-stroma-enriched genes were linked to extracellular matrix (ECM)
organization, cell adhesion, and tissue remodeling, suggesting a
matrix-constructive phenotype. Hierarchical clustering reinforced the
spatial segregation of CAF transcriptional programs, outlining potential
targets for overcoming drug resistance in HGSC.

**Conclusions and perspectives**

Our analysis identified potential fibroblast targets to address drug
resistance and underscored the value of spatial profiling in resolving
differences between distant and TSI fibroblasts. Future work will focus
on functional validation of selected candidates in immune-competent
patient-derived cell (icPDC) co-culture models to determine their roles
in tumor progression and therapy response.

lobtype=pdf The first method, minced tissue method (Section 3.1.1), is based on the capability of fibroblasts to crawl out of tissue samples, thus facilitating their harvest. Minced tissue method provides fairly homogenous fibroblastic cell-cultures. Therefore, this method assures the isolation of a variety of different subpopulations of fibroblasts present in a given (normal or tumor-associated) tissue. The second method, enzymatic digestion method (Section 3.1.2), is faster and yields a higher recovery rate of cells from the tissue.

Data analysis #5 (meeting 24.06.2025)

**PDGFRB and PDGFRA:**

Some studies report that **high stromal PDGFRB** correlates with **poor
prognosis**, but findings are **inconsistent**.

Based on IHC of PDGFRB and PDGFRA in stromal and tumor compartments in
HGSC, PDGFRA and PDGFRB **doesn't have** prognostic benefit in HGSC
\[1\].

**FAP:**

igh stromal PDGFRB** correlates with **poor
prognosis**, but findings are **inconsistent**.

Based on IHC of PDGFRB and PDGFRA in stromal and tumor compartments in
HGSC, PDGFRA and PDGFRB **doesn't have** prognostic benefit in HGSC
\[1\].

**FAP:**

**High FAP⁺ fibroblast abundance** alone **was not** associated with
shorter survival **in the whole cohort** \[2\].

**However**, when **both CD8⁺ T-cell density and FAP⁺ fibroblast levels
were high**, outcomes were **significantly worse**:

- In the **discovery cohort**:

 - High FAP + high CD8 → **shorter PFS** (11.4 vs. 18.6 months, *p*
 > = 0.01)

- In the **validation cohort**:

 - High FAP + high CD8 → **shorter OS** (31.5 vs. 76.9 months, *p*
 > = 0.02)

### **Spatial Immune Exclusion in TSI and Distant Stroma**

Analysis of **cell abundances** in the TSI and distant stroma segments
reveals **spatial exclusion of immune cells**, likely mediated by
**myCAFs** present in the TSI. Specifically, the proportions of **B
cells, NK cells, dendritic cells, CD8⁺ and CD4⁺ T cells, and endothelial
cells** are significantly reduced in the TSI.

This analysis and corresponding plot were performed **for IDS samples
only**.

![](media/image68.png){width="6.5in" height="4.333333333333333in"}

**TROP2 in TME**

The following analysis was performed on both PDS and IDS samples.\
First, we conducted a basic exploration to determine the cellular source
of **TROP2 expression**. Our results indicate that TROP2 expression is
**restricted to tumor cells**. Specifically:

- TROP2 expression is significantly **higher in tumor AOIs** compared
 > to non-tumor regions.

ion to determine the cellular source
of **TROP2 expression**. Our results indicate that TROP2 expression is
**restricted to tumor cells**. Specifically:

- TROP2 expression is significantly **higher in tumor AOIs** compared
 > to non-tumor regions.

- There is a **positive correlation** between the proportion of tumor
 > cells within an AOI and TROP2 expression levels.

- Additionally, **deconvolution analysis** confirms that the signal
 > attributed to tumor cells is substantially higher than that from
 > other cell types.

These findings suggest that **TROP2 is predominantly expressed by tumor
cells** within the tumor microenvironment.

height="3.540573053368329in"}

![](media/image27.png){width="6.5in" height="3.4027777777777777in"}

### **TROP2 and Immune Cell Proportions in Tumor AOIs**

TROP2 expression is associated with **lower proportions of immune
cells** within tumor AOIs. Overall, the proportions of various immune
cell types are **inversely correlated** with TROP2 expression,
suggesting that tumor cells may **spatially exclude immune cells** from
the tumor microenvironment.

An important avenue for further investigation is the potential
relationship between **TROP2 expression in tumor cells** and **immune
exhaustion**, which may provide additional insight into tumor-immune
interaction

# Sample S460_iOme 

**17.06.2024 - Day 0**

**Fibroblast isolation**

Small tissue pieces were placed in a 6-well plate. Approx. 6 pieces per
well and with 800 ul media per well. No digestion was used.

**18.06.2024 - Day 1**

300 ul medium was added on day 1.

**24.06.2024 - Day 7**

The tissue pieces were removed from the wells as enough fibroblasts had
isolated and attached to the plate. Wells were washed once with PDB and
2 ml fresh media was added.

Media was changed every Mon, Wed, and Fri. Before the media change,
wells were washed once with PBS.

**30.06.2024 - Day 13**

Wells 1 and 2 were trypsinized (0.25% trypsin EDTA, 3 min at +37C) and
pooled together for plating. All of these cells were plated in a cell
culture plate with 5 ml of fresh media.

→ Plate 1: S460 iOme P1 w/o dig. DMEM/F12+++ +10 % FBS 30.06.24 MH

**01.07.2024 - Day 14**

Wells 3, 4, and 5 were trypsinized and pooled together for plating.
Cells were resuspended into 1 ml media and 750 µl of the cell suspension
was plated into a cell culture plate with 5 ml of fresh media.

→ Plate 2: S460 iOme P1 w/o dig. DMEM/F12+++ +10 % FBS 01.07.2024 AE

**02.07.2024 - Day 15**

**Coverslip preparation**

**A**utoclaved coverslips were placed in the wells of a 24-well plate. 6
wells were coated with Cultrex gel solution, 4 wells were coated with
Omentum gel solution, and 6 wells were left uncoated. All reagents were
kept on ice during preparation.

The final concentration of both gels was determined to be 0.5 mg/ml. See
the calculation for gel solutions below.

Cultrex gel solution was prepared by mixing media and Cultrex gel stock

t uncoated. All reagents were
kept on ice during preparation.

The final concentration of both gels was determined to be 0.5 mg/ml. See
the calculation for gel solutions below.

Cultrex gel solution was prepared by mixing media and Cultrex gel stock
and left on ice for a few minutes before adding it to the wells. 400 µl
of Cultrex gel solution was added per well. Cultrex gel solution needs
to incubate overnight at +37°C, and the coating solution can be removed
just before cell seeding.

Omentum gel solution was prepared by first adding omentum stock,
aprotinin, and thrombin to the media. After fibrinogen was thawed and
warmed in a +37°C water bath and everything was ready for plating the
omentum gel solution, fibrinogen was added to the solution.

We tried adding 400 µl of Omentum gel solution per well, but it was too
much as it covered the whole well, not just the coverslip. We did new
calculations (seen below) and added 100 µl of Omentum gel solution per
well. That was enough to cover the coverslip with a thin layer of the
gel. However, even less could be enough. After 1 hour of incubation at
+37°C, medium was added to the Omentum gel-coated wells.

400 µl PBS was added to the uncoated coverslips/wells.

Anti-fibroblast MicroBeads protocol by Miltenyi Biotec was used as
guidance.

MACS buffer was prepared in advance: 49.55 ml DPBS (w/o MgCl2 and
CaCl2) + 250 µl FBS + 200 µl EDTA) to have 0.5 % FBS and 2 mM EDTA in
DPBS. MACS buffer was prepared sterile and everything else was sterile
except for EDTA. The prepared buffer was sterile-filtered before using
it.

MACS buffer, cells, and beads were kept on ice when not needed inside
the hood.

After calculations cells were centrifuged at 300 g for 10 min at +4C.
The supernatant was aspirated and the cell pellet was resuspended in 80
µl of cold MACS buffer (commercial protocol suggests 80 µl per 10 E7
cells). To the suspension, 20 µl of Anti-Fibroblast MicroBeads was added
(protocol: 20 µl per 10 E7 cells) Cell suspension was mixed well and
incubated 15 min at +4C (protocol: 30 min at RT). Cells were washed by
adding 1 ml MACS buffer and centrifuged 300 g for 10 min at +4C. The
supernatant was aspirated and cells were resuspended in 500 µl of MACS
buffer.

LS column and 30 µm filter were placed in the magnetic MACS Separator.
Column and filter were prepared by washing it with 3 ml MACS buffer. The
cell suspension was added to the column and washed with 3 x 3 ml MACS
buffer. Collect flow-through to a separate 15 ml falcon tube, this time
cell suspension and washing were collected into the same falcon tube.

The column was removed from the separator and placed on a new 15 ml
falcon tube. 5 ml MACS buffer was added to the column and with firmly
pushing the plunger into the column, magnetically labeled cells flushed
out.

Both cell suspensions were centrifuged 300 x for 10 min and then

**26.07.2024 - day 39**

**IF staining of PFA fixed cells in coverslips**

Firstly, prepared Blocking buffer (3 % BSA and 2 % TritonX-100 in 1xPBS)
by adding 0.3 g of BSA and 0.2 ml TritonX-100 into 10 ml 1xPBS. To
enable the pipeting of triton, cutted the smalles part of a pipet tip
off. Vortex the solution thoroughly until BSA and triton was dissolved.

Prepared a IF chamber with wet tissue papers and parafilm, and added

2 ml TritonX-100 into 10 ml 1xPBS. To
enable the pipeting of triton, cutted the smalles part of a pipet tip
off. Vortex the solution thoroughly until BSA and triton was dissolved.

Prepared a IF chamber with wet tissue papers and parafilm, and added
coverslips on top to the parafilm. Added 50 µl of the blocking buffer
per coverslip and incubated 30 min RT shielded from light. Removed
blocking buffer by converting the coverslip and tipeting the buffer from
the edge of the coverslip and from the parafilm.

Prepared antibodymix with 1.75 µl PAX8, 1.167 µl FAP and 0.12 µl aSMA
antibodies in 350 µl blocking buffer. Added 50 µl of the antibodymix to
the coverslip and incubated 45 min RT shielded from light. Removed the
antibody by converting the coverslip and washed them by pipetting 100 µl
PBS onto the coverslips while folding them converted. Prepared HOECHST
antibodymix with 35 µl of 1:100 diluted HOECHST mix into 350 µl blocking
buffer. Added 50 µl of HOECHST mix per coverslip and incubated 5 min.

After incubation, washed the coverslips by pipetting 100 µl PBS onto the
coverslips while folding them converted. Removed excess PBS by turning
the coverslip sideways on a paper tissue. Mounted the coverslips by
adding 20 µl glycerol onto the coverslip and placing the coverslips on
to microscopy slides.

of the medium and counted.

Surface staining:

Added FACS buffer and centrifuged 400 g +4 C 6 min.

Made an antibody mix for the multicolor samples; 50µl per tube, diluted
in Brilliant Stain buffer. For FMO samples, added the needed amount
Brilliant Stain buffer (see table below) to the cells and then added
antibodies one by one to the tubes (containing the cells). For FMO
samples, every other antibody was added except the corresponding one.

Incubated all samples 30 min at +4C and an additional 10 min RT. Washed
and incubated for 40 min with Fixation buffer (#00-8222 Invitrogen) and
added 1x permeabilization (10x perm (Invitrogen) buffer diluted in
mQH2O) for 5 min. Centrifuged and resuspended the pellets in FACS buffer
for overnight storage at +4C.

## Flow cytometry results

Some cells are outside of the scale but the dissociation worked better
(compared to S464_pOvaR) and we can clearly see more stromal cells in
the scatter plot. From EpCAM-, CD45- and CD31- cells, plots with CD29 to
FAP and a-SMA to CD29 show similar patter as seen in Givel et al paper
[[https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-018-03348-z]{.underline}](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-018-03348-z).

Conclusion is that FSC and SSC voltages need to be smaller. Maybe FSC
150 to 100 and SSC 230 to 210.

From Givel et al: Figure 1. d Gating strategy to identify CAF subsets in
HGSOC by FACS. Results from a representative HGSOC patient are shown.
Cells isolated from freshly dissociated human HGSOC were first gated on
DAPI−, EPCAM−, CD45−, CD31− cells, for excluding dead cells (DAPI+),
epithelial cells (EPCAM+), hematopoietic cells (CD45+), and endothelial
cells (CD31+). Selected cells were next examined using six fibroblast
markers. Representative flow cytometry plots show gating strategies
based on FAP, CD29, SMA, and FSP1 that allow the identification of four
sub-populations of fibroblasts in HGSOC: CAF-S1 are CD29Med FAPHigh
SMAHigh FSP1High, CAF-S4 are CD29High FAPLow SMAHigh FSP1Med, CAF-S3 are
CD29Low FAPLow SMALow FSP1Med/High and CAF-S2 are CD29Low FAPLow SMALow
FSP1Low.

Experimental Steps:

1\. \*Sample Preparation\*:

- Collected ovarian tumor samples and washed the larger tumor pieces
 > by placing them into a 50 ml Falcon tube, pouring sterile HBSS on
 > top, and gently inverting the tube several times.

- Approx. ⅔ of the tumor pieces were sliced and divided into two 50 ml
 > Falcon tubes for conditions 1&2 and 3&4.

- The remaining ⅓ of the tumor pieces were minced into smaller pieces
 > using a sterile scalpel and scissors, and then transferred into a
 > 50 ml Falcon tube for conditions 5 and 6.

2\. \*Dissociation Protocols\*:

\*Collagenase & hyaluronidase-only conditions\*:

- Treated 1&2 and 3&4 with 0.125 % trypsin for 20 min in +37°C water
 > bath.

 > 10 ml 0.25% trypsin EDTA

 - Gently inverted the tubes a few times after 10 min and at the
 > end of the incubation period.

- After incubation, took the tissue pieces out and placed them into
 > two cell culture dishes for mincing. To the two cell suspension,
 > added 20 ml DMEM/F12+++ media each, centrifuged 300 g 5 min RT,
 > and resuspended both tubes in 1 ml. Pooled the cell suspension
 > together and marked this cell suspension as sample 1-4.

- Minced the sliced tissue pieces into tiny pieces, and divided the
 > contents of the two plates into two 50 ml Falcon tubes each.

**Fibroblast purification**

Tried again fibroblast purification for cells fron conditions 1-5 pooled
together. Anti-fibroblast MicroBeads protocol by Miltenyi Biotec was
used as guidance.

Used the previously prepared MACS buffer, but re- sterile-filtered
before using it.

MACS buffer, cells, and beads were kept on ice when not needed inside
the hood.

After calculations cells were centrifuged at 300 g for 5 min RT. The
supernatant was aspirated and the cell pellet was resuspended in 80 µl
of cold MACS buffer (Commercial protocol suggests 80 µl per 10 E7 cells.
Our cell count was much less than E7, but decided to try this for now
and next time could decrease the volume of the buffer and beads.) To the
suspension, 20 µl of Anti-Fibroblast MicroBeads was added (protocol: 20

ol suggests 80 µl per 10 E7 cells.
Our cell count was much less than E7, but decided to try this for now
and next time could decrease the volume of the buffer and beads.) To the
suspension, 20 µl of Anti-Fibroblast MicroBeads was added (protocol: 20
µl per 10 E7 cells) Cell suspension was mixed well and incubated 30 min
at RT. Cells were washed by adding 1 ml MACS buffer and centrifuged 300
g for 10 min. The supernatant was aspirated and cells were resuspended
in 500 µl of MACS buffer.

LS column and 30 µm filter were placed in the magnetic MACS Separator.
Column and filter were prepared by washing it with 3 ml MACS buffer. The
cell suspension was added to the column and washed with 3 x 3 ml MACS
buffer. Collected the flow-through to a 15 ml falcon tube which should
include all cells except fibroblasts. The column was removed from the
separator and placed on a new 15 ml falcon tube. 5 ml MACS buffer was
added to the column and with firmly pushing the plunger into the column,
magnetically labeled cells flushed out. Both cell suspensions were
centrifuged 300 g for 10 min, and resuspended in 500 µl of DMEM/F12+++
media.

# Sample S473_pOme 

**05.08.2024 - day 0 and 06.08.2024 - day 1**

**Copied from Dissociation_protocol_optimization_S473_pOme:**

Approaches:

1\. \*Using trypsin and collagenase & hyaluronidase\*:

- Condition 1: 30 min 0.125 % trypsin and 1 hour C&H

- Condition 3: 30 min 0.125 % trypsin, 1 hour C&H, followed by brief
 > (15 sec) 0.125% trypsin of the cell pellet

2\. \*Using dispase followed by collagenase & hyaluronidase\*:

- Condition 6: Dispase for 30 min, followed by C&H for 1 hour

Experimental Steps:

1\. \*Sample Preparation\*:

- Collected ovarian tumor samples and washed the larger tumor pieces
 > by placing them into a 50 ml Falcon tube, pouring sterile HBSS on
 > top, and gently inverting the tube several times.

- The tumor piece was cut in three adjacent parts

- Two pieces were added into a 50 ml Falcon tube containing 20 mL of
 > 0.125 % trypsin in PBS for conditions 1 and 3.

- The remaining piece was minced into small pieces using a sterile
 > scalpel and scissors, and then transferred into a 50 mL Falcon
 > tube for condition 6.

2\.

0 ml Falcon tube containing 20 mL of
 > 0.125 % trypsin in PBS for conditions 1 and 3.

- The remaining piece was minced into small pieces using a sterile
 > scalpel and scissors, and then transferred into a 50 mL Falcon
 > tube for condition 6.

2\. \*Dissociation Protocols\*:

\*Collagenase & hyaluronidase-only conditions\*:

- For condition 1&3, treated with 0.125 % trypsin for 30 min in +37°C
 > water bath.

 - 0.125 % trypsin solution: 10 ml DPBS (without MgCl2 and CaCl2) +
 > 10 ml 0.25% trypsin EDTA

 - Gently inverted the tubes a few times every 10 min and at the
 > end of the incubation period.

- After incubation, took the tissue pieces out and placed them into a
 > cell culture dishes for mincing.

- To the cell suspension, added 20 ml DMEM/F12+++ media, centrifuged
 > 1000 rpm 10 min +4C, did RBC lysis (2 ml of lysis buffer, 5 min
 > incuction and diluted with 8 ml of media) and resuspended in 1 ml
 > media.

- Minced the sliced tissue pieces into tiny pieces, and divided the
 > contents into two 50 ml Falcon tubes (condition 1 and condition
 > 3).

- Treated samples with C&H for 1 hour at +37°C rotating. Vortexed
 > after the incubation period.

 - C&H dissociation media per condition: 4.45 ml DMEM/F12+++
 > media + 500 µl C&H (10x stock) + 50 µl DNAse (RNAse free)

- Filtered the samples with a 70 µm cell strainer to obtain
 > single-cell suspensions

 - Pre-wetted the filter with 1 ml DMEM/F12+++, transferred the
 > digested suspension through the strainer into a 50 ml Falcon
 > tube, washed the stainer with 5 ml media, and discarded the
 > leftover tissue pieces. Centrifuged the cell suspension 10 min
 > 1000 rpm at +4°C.

- Red blood cell lysis:

 - 3 ml RBC lysis buffer, incubated 5 min at RT, 12 ml DMEM/F12+++

Falcon
 > tube, washed the stainer with 5 ml media, and discarded the
 > leftover tissue pieces. Centrifuged the cell suspension 10 min
 > 1000 rpm at +4°C.

- Red blood cell lysis:

 - 3 ml RBC lysis buffer, incubated 5 min at RT, 12 ml DMEM/F12+++
 > on top and centrifuged 10 min 1000 rpm at +4°C.

- For condition 3, added 0.125% trypsin and incubate for 15 seconds.

- RBC lysis: 4 ml, incubated 5 min RT, added 20 ml cold DMEM/F12+++
 > media, centrifuged

- Resuspended in 2 ml of DMEM/F12 media and made a 1:10 dilution for
 > counting. Counted the cells and prepared the aliquots for flow
 > cytometry, and freezing.

\*Dispase followed by collagenase conditions\*:

 > Vortexed before and after the incubation period.

 > 50 (U/ml stock) + 100 µl DNAse (RNAse free)

- Filter the samples to obtain single-cell suspensions. **Keep the
 > tissue leftovers.**

3\. \*Fibroblast enrichment\*:

Rest of dispase + C&H dissociated cells was used for fibroblast
enrichment. The total cell count was 42.2 E6 cells before starting the
protocol.

Anti-fibroblast MicroBeads protocol by Miltenyi Biotec was used as
guidance.

Used the previously prepared MACS buffer, but re- sterile-filtered
before using it.

MACS buffer, cells, and beads were kept on ice when not needed inside
the hood.

Cells were centrifuged at 300 g for 5 min RT and the cell pellet was
resuspended in 80 µl of cold MACS buffer (Commercial protocol suggests

e-filtered
before using it.

MACS buffer, cells, and beads were kept on ice when not needed inside
the hood.

Cells were centrifuged at 300 g for 5 min RT and the cell pellet was
resuspended in 80 µl of cold MACS buffer (Commercial protocol suggests
80 µl per 10 E7 cells. To the suspension, 20 µl of Anti-Fibroblast
MicroBeads was added (protocol: 20 µl per 10 E7 cells) Cell suspension
was mixed well and incubated 30 min at RT. Cells were washed by adding 1
ml MACS buffer and centrifuged 300 g for 10 min. The supernatant was
aspirated and cells were resuspended in 500 µl of MACS buffer.

LS column and 30 µm filter were placed in the magnetic MACS Separator.
Column and filter were prepared by washing it with 3 ml MACS buffer. The
cell suspension was added to the column and washed with 3 x 3 ml MACS
buffer. Collected the flow-through to a 15 ml falcon tube which should
include all cells except fibroblasts. The column was removed from the
separator and placed on a new 15 ml falcon tube. 5 ml MACS buffer was
added to the column and with firmly pushing the plunger into the column,
magnetically labeled cells flushed out. Both cell suspensions were
centrifuged 300 g for 10 min, and resuspended in 500 µl of DMEM/F12+++
media.

1\. \*Sample Preparation\*:

 > stock) + 100 µl DNAse (RNAse free)

- Filtered the samples to obtain single-cell suspensions: 70 µm cell

 > digested suspension through the strainer into a 50 ml Falcon

 > leftover tissue pieces.

- RBC lysis: 2 ml, incubated 5 min RT, added 10 ml cold DMEM/F12+++
 > media, centrifuged

- Resuspended in 1 ml of DMEM/F12 media and made a 1:10 dilution for
 > counting. Counted the cells and prepared the aliquots for flow
 > cytometry.

\*Dispase followed by collagenase conditions\*:

- Treated sample with dispase for 30 minutes (at +37°C rotating).
 > Vortexed before and after the incubation period.

 - dispase dissociation media: 9.7 ml DMEM/F12+++ + 200 µl dispase
 > 50 (U/ml stock) + 100 µl DNAse (RNAse free)

**Experimental Plan**

Experiment: Dissociation protocol optimization **S488_iOme**

Objective: Optimize the dissociation protocol to isolate more
fibroblasts from ovarian tumors.

Approaches:

1\. \*Using trypsin and collagenase & hyaluronidase\*:

1 hour C&H

2\. \*Using dispase followed by collagenase & hyaluronidase\*:

30 min dispase + 1 hour C&H

Experimental Steps:

1\. \*Sample Preparation\*:

- Collected ovarian tumor samples

- Washed big tissue pieces twice with HBSS.

- Minced the tumor piece into small pieces using a sterile scalpel and

passaging protocol".

6 wells.

While splitting, kept the organoids from 2 different wells separately.
Prepared also 2 vials with diluted Cultrex: to achieve 7.5 mg/ml
concentration of Cultrex, diluted 481 µl of stock (11.7 mg/ml) into 269
µl cold DPBS. Both diluted Cultrex solutions should have contained over
750 µl but when pipetting took everything (which was under 750 µl) and
added that into the cell pellet. Made 3 x 10 droplets of the first

d 481 µl of stock (11.7 mg/ml) into 269
µl cold DPBS. Both diluted Cultrex solutions should have contained over

# 22.10.2024 Fibroblast culture optimization

Splitted S488_iOme P4(1/8 P3) after dispase and C&H dissociation
7.10.2024 to 1:6. Kept one plate: S488_iOme P5(1/6 P4) after dispase and

Made first version of a protocol to start comparing fibroblasts in
different culturing conditions. Based on cell count, decided to try
cells in 3 different densities: 0.5 E6 cells/ml, 1 E6 cells/ml and 2 E6
cells/ml. Different conditions that were chosen to this experiment were:
cells centrifuged, cells resuspended, Cultrex 2.5 mg/ml centrifuged,
Cultrex 2.5 mg/ml not centrifuged, Cultrex 7.5 mg/ml centrifuged and
Cultrex 7.5 mg/ml not centrifuged.

Cells centrifuged means that after the cell suspension is plated, the
plate is centrifuged and then pellet not resuspended into media. Cultrex
centrifuged means that the droplets are made as usually but after plated
the plate is centrifuged.

Used 2 plates of S488_iOme_P4(1/8 P3) after dispase+C&H dissociation
7.10.2024 AE

Cells were plated on Corning® 96-well Clear Round Bottom Ultra-Low
Attachment Microplate, Individually Wrapped, with Lid, Sterile, Product
Number7007

After the 45 min but before adding the media, could clearly see cells as
a pellet in all the centrifuged wells. In addition, the cells in media
that was not centifuged were at the bottom of the u-bottom. However,
after I added the media, the cells "disappeared" meaning that they were
not clearly in the bottom of the well and hard to focus on. The media
was added really carefully along the well wall, so the cells and the
matrix (Cultrex) are definitely in the well.

Suggestion for next time: Cultrex could be diluted with media so there
is no need to add media on top of the droplets.

Next day - 23.10.2024 took pictures of the wells: Cells without matrix
were easy to find and clearly in the bottom of the wells. However, cells
in Cultrex were still hard to find in most of the wells. Especially in
wells with cells in Cultrex 7.5 mg/ml and centrifuged, cells appeared to
be in the top parts of the well as the microscopy could not focus high
enough. Also, you could see something flowing in the cultrex wells.

**Copied from Dissociation_protocol_optimization**

Experiment: Dissociation protocol optimization **S494_pOme**

Objective: Optimize the dissociation protocol to isolate more
fibroblasts from ovarian tumors.

S494_pOme sample was processed together with S495_pOme

Approaches:

1\. \*Using trypsin and collagenase & hyaluronidase\*:

1 hour C&H

2\. \*Using dispase followed by collagenase & hyaluronidase\*:

30 min dispase + 1 hour C&H

Experimental Steps:

1\. \*Sample Preparation\*:

- Collected ovarian tumor samples

- Washed big tissue pieces once with HBSS.

- Minced the tumor piece into small pieces using a sterile scalpel and
 > scissors, and divided the minced tissue into two equal parts.

2\. \*Dissociation Protocols\*:

\*Collagenase & hyaluronidase-only conditions\*:

- Treated sample with C&H for 1 hour at +37°C rotating. Vortexed
 > before and after the incubation period.

 - C&H dissociation media: 8.9 ml DMEM/F12+++ media + 1 ml C&H (10x
 > stock) + 100 µl DNAse (RNAse free)

- Filtered the samples to obtain single-cell suspensions: 70 µm cell
 > strainer

 - Pre-wetted the filter with 1 ml DMEM/F12+++, transfer the
 > digested suspension through the strainer into a 50 ml Falcon
 > tube, wash the stainer with 5 ml media, and discard the
 > leftover tissue pieces.

 - Centrifuge the cell suspension 10 min 1000 rpm at +4°C

- RBC lysis: 3 ml, incubated 5 min RT, added 15 ml cold DMEM/F12+++
 > media, centrifuged

- Resuspended in 1 ml of DMEM/F12 media and prepared a 1:10 dilution
 > for counting.

issue pieces.

 - Centrifuge the cell suspension 10 min 1000 rpm at +4°C

- RBC lysis: 3 ml, incubated 5 min RT, added 15 ml cold DMEM/F12+++
 > media, centrifuged

- Resuspended in 1 ml of DMEM/F12 media and prepared a 1:10 dilution
 > for counting. Counted the cells and prepared the aliquots for flow
 > cytometry.

\*Dispase followed by collagenase conditions\*:

- Treated sample with dispase for 30 minutes (at +37°C rotating).
 > Vortexed before and after the incubation period.

 - dispase dissociation media: 9.7 ml DMEM/F12+++ + 200 µl dispase
 > 50 (U/ml stock) + 100 µl DNAse (RNAse free)

- Filter the samples to obtain single-cell suspensions. **Keep the
 > tissue leftovers.**

- **For cell suspension (dispase only):**

 - Centrifuged the cell suspension 10 min 1000 rpm at +4°C

 - Then, it was resuspended in 1 ml media to wait for the other
 > conditions to be at the same step. Then, centrifuged again,
 > and continued with RBC lysis.

 - RBC lysis: 2 ml, incubated 5 min RT, added 10 ml cold
 > DMEM/F12+++ media, centrifuged

 - Resuspended in 1 ml of DMEM/F12 media, and prepared a 1:10
 > dilution for counting. Counted the cells and prepared the
 > aliquots for flow cytometry, and freezing.

- Further, the leftover tissue was dissociated with C&H for 1 h at
 > +37°C rotating. Vortexed before and after the incubation period.

 - C&H dissociation media: 8.9 ml DMEM/F12+++ media + 1 ml C&H (10x
 > stock) + 100 µl DNAse (RNAse free)

- Filtered the samples to obtain single-cell suspensions

- RBC lysis: 3 ml, incubated 5 min RT, added 15 ml cold DMEM/F12+++
 > media, centrifuged

- Resuspended in 1 ml of DMEM/F12 media, and prepared a 1:10 dilution
 > for counting. Counted the cells and prepared the aliquots for flow
 > cytometry.

**Experimental Plan for Multiplexed scRNA-seq (Main Discussion, \~40
min)**

**Technical Considerations & Requirements:**

- **There is a new scRNA-seq method** that allows loading **up to
 > 40,000 cells if needed**; standard is 10,000.

- Goal: load **30,000 cells**, to sequence **20,000**.

(Main Discussion, \~40
min)**

**Technical Considerations & Requirements:**

- **There is a new scRNA-seq method** that allows loading **up to
 > 40,000 cells if needed**; standard is 10,000.

- Goal: load **30,000 cells**, to sequence **20,000**.

- Start with **50,000--100,000 cells/condition**.

- Ideal input for the droplet generation: **150,000 cells total**, to
 > account for possible losses and errors during the prep.

- **Cell concentration**: \~1700--2000 live cells/µL.

**Multiplexing Strategy**

**Hashtag C TotalSeq antibodies** support 5\' end sequencing (TCR/BCR
compatible).

- TotalSeq A, B, and C available; C fits 5' end sequencing.

- Antibodies \~450 EUR/antibody; use **2 µL per 10⁶ cells/sample**.

- See: [[BioLegend
 > TotalSeq]{.underline}](https://www.biolegend.com/nl-nl/totalseq/single-cell-rna/multiplex-samples-and-hashtags)

- **Including TCR sequencing does not increase the costs dramatically,
 > might be a very nice option to consider for an extra layer of
 > information.**

- **Number of multiplexed conditions**: 3--5 per sample preferred
 > (Satu multiplexes up to 10 but with cell lines).

- **Avoid using UMI barcode #11** due to known issues.

- **Dead cell removal is recommended**, especially if viability is
 > \~40--50%.

- However, they\'ve successfully run 20--40% viability samples.

- **Fixation of hashed cells**: not tested/validated in their setup.

- It might be possible to pool patients when multiplexing different
 > treatment conditions using either 1) SNPs for patient
 > demultiplexing or 2) UMIs (since the number of treatments will not
 > exceed the number of available UMI-labelled antibodies combos).

- **Alternative multiplexing** method available: **chip-/bead-based**,
 > less time-consuming than hashing.

ient
 > demultiplexing or 2) UMIs (since the number of treatments will not
 > exceed the number of available UMI-labelled antibodies combos).

and one anti-FRa

There are risks associated to both of these options:

1\) Maybe we don't see a significant difference between the two
conditions.

2\) FRa-targeted ADCs have only proven effective in selected patients
with very high FRa expression. Since our pilot is a cohort of only 4
patients, the risk that only one is FRa high is quite high, and
therefore we would be testing the therapy mostly in patients where it
wouldn't be effective

If we don't see a lot of differences between the two anti-TROP ADCs, the
results would still be publishable, as we can report that they still
work and that the effects on the TME cells are x and y. On the other
hand, we could also test anti-FRa and see if the combination with ICI is
synergistic, which is also interesting and publishable. However, this
seems more risky. Also, the clinical relevance would be higher if we
test anti-TROP2 because anyways the anti-FRa is only approved and most
likely reimbursed to patients that are selected through the FRa
biomarker (very high expression), so our results most likely wouldn't
change this. **We will then include both of the anti-TROP2 ADCs, and
keep the anti-FRa as backup**

We can still do flow cytometry paired with the scRNA-seq and look at the
immune cell activation upon treatment as we are also testing the
possible synergy of ADCs in combination with immunotherapy.

**We need to determine the concentrations of the ADCs we want to use. To
do this, we will perform titration experiments.** Our primary endpoint
is cell survival. Although the goal of ADCs in patients is to kill
cancer cells, in our experiments we need to identify the concentration
at which the drug begins to have an effect, but the cells still survive
until the readout.

#### Notes

**Experimental Plan for Multiplexed scRNA-seq (Main Discussion, \~40
min)**

**Technical Considerations & Requirements:**

- **There is a new scRNA-seq method** that allows loading **up to
 > 40,000 cells if needed**; standard is 10,000.

- Goal: load **30,000 cells**, to sequence **20,000**.

- Start with **50,000--100,000 cells/condition**.

- Ideal input for the droplet generation: **150,000 cells total**, to
 > account for possible losses and errors during the prep.

- **Cell concentration**: \~1700--2000 live cells/µL.

**Multiplexing Strategy**

**Hashtag C TotalSeq antibodies** support 5\' end sequencing (TCR/BCR
compatible).

- TotalSeq A, B, and C available; C fits 5' end sequencing.

- Antibodies \~450 EUR/antibody; use **2 µL per 10⁶ cells/sample**.

- See: [[BioLegend
 > TotalSeq]{.underline}](https://www.biolegend.com/nl-nl/totalseq/single-cell-rna/multiplex-samples-and-hashtags)

- **Including TCR sequencing does not increase the costs dramatically,
 > might be a very nice option to consider for an extra layer of
 > information.**

- **Number of multiplexed conditions**: 3--5 per sample preferred
 > (Satu multiplexes up to 10 but with cell lines).

- **Avoid using UMI barcode #11** due to known issues.

- **Dead cell removal is recommended**, especially if viability is
 > \~40--50%.

- However, they\'ve successfully run 20--40% viability samples.

- **Fixation of hashed cells**: not tested/validated in their setup.

- It might be possible to pool patients when multiplexing different
 > treatment conditions using either 1) SNPs for patient
 > demultiplexing or 2) UMIs (since the number of treatments will not

hashed cells**: not tested/validated in their setup.

- It might be possible to pool patients when multiplexing different
 > treatment conditions using either 1) SNPs for patient
 > demultiplexing or 2) UMIs (since the number of treatments will not
 > exceed the number of available UMI-labelled antibodies combos).

- **Alternative multiplexing** method available: **chip-/bead-based**,
 > less time-consuming than hashing. However, hashing might be the
 > best for us, especially if we can multiplex patients with the
 > UMI-labelled antibodies as well.

**Sample processing strategy**

- Cell counts should be done before and after hashing and preparation.

- Viability check is critical before proceeding.

**Cost Estimates & Logistics**

- Pilot study is highly encouraged (e.g. 3 patients ± fibroblasts).
 > Ideally, also include the drugs that will be tested to get a sense
 > of the impact in viability.

- Library prep reagents: price to be clarified (Basel will ask their

Insights into tumor-host interactions in high-grade serous ovarian cancer using a 3D model of the tumor ecosystem
INTRODUCTION
High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) is the most lethal form of ovarian cancer, with treatment resistance often driven by complex interactions within the tumor microenvironment (TME). Conventional 2D models lack the cellular diversity and structural context needed to study these interactions, limiting their ability to predict patient responses. To address this, we developed a patient-derived 3D model of HGSC that includes cancer cells, immune cells, and cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) embedded in a human omentum-derived extracellular matrix (ECM). This model better recapitulates the TME and provides a platform to explore drug responses and identify predictive biomarkers.

Cancer-associated fibroblasts condition the ovarian cancer microenvironment and therapeutic responses to antibody-drug conjugates and immunotherapy
High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) tumors are complex ecosystems where tumor–stromal–immune interactions shape therapeutic responses.

ion the ovarian cancer microenvironment and therapeutic responses to antibody-drug conjugates and immunotherapy
High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) tumors are complex ecosystems where tumor–stromal–immune interactions shape therapeutic responses. Cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) are major regulators in this dynamic crosstalk, yet their spatially distinct functional roles remain poorly understood. Using single-cell and spatial transcriptomics alongside patient-derived ex vivo models, we are mapping how CAF programs are organized at the HGSC tumor–stroma interface (TSI), and how this organization influences therapy sensitivity.

Cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) are major regulators in this dynamic crosstalk, yet their spatially distinct functional roles remain poorly understood. ## Slide 6
Aino’s (Thesis) gating strategy:
Exclude debris: Gate out small particles or cell fragments that are not relevant to the analysis.

Select single cells: Ensure only individual cells are analyzed, excluding doublets or aggregates.

Gate live cells: Exclude dead cells using the live/dead marker.

Identify major populations using:

EpCAM⁺ → Cancer cells
CD45⁺ → Leukocytes
EpCAM⁻ CD45⁻ → Non-immune stroma
EpCAM- CD45- CD31+ → Endothelial cells
EpCAM- CD45- CD31- FAP+/a-SMA+/PDRGF-b+/CD29+ → Cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs)

With therapeutic antibodies gating is straightforward, but not with ADCs … so, we could use:

## Slide 8
Possible ADC readout: Toxicity assay
Tumor cells should be the primary ADC target

But ADCs may cause a bystander effect, where the payload diffuses into nearby non-target cells (CAF, immune cells), so we want to detect also cell death/apoptosis 

Inside each population, we could gate for:

cells should be the primary ADC target

But ADCs may cause a bystander effect, where the payload diffuses into nearby non-target cells (CAF, immune cells), so we want to detect also cell death/apoptosis 

Inside each population, we could gate for:

X axis: Annexin V (e.g., AF700) (Note: Annexin V Binding Buffer (contains Ca²⁺))
Y axis: DCM (viability dye)

And then interpret Quadrants as:

Annexin− / DCM− → Live (healthy)
Annexin+ / DCM− → Early apoptotic
Annexin+ / DCM+ → Late apoptotic / necrotic
Annexin− / DCM+ → Necrotic / membrane-compromised

Cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs)
3
Created with BioRender.com
Speaker notes: Within the TME, CAFs are a highly heterogenous population

A high amounts of CAFs in epithelial ovarian cancer tumors is linked to advanced-stage disease such as the presence of lymph node and omentum metastases. 

CAFs contribute to tumor growth and behavior by promoting metastasis and angiogenesis, as well as affecting immune cell infiltration and modulating immune responses

Therefore there is a need to have a model that represents the TME and which incorporates CAFs

## Slide 9
Gating strategy for flow cytometry analysis
9
EpCAM+ = Cancer cells
CD45+ = Leukocytes
EpCAM- CD45- = Non-immune stroma
EpCAM- CD45- CD31+ = Endothelial cells
EpCAM- CD45- CD31- FAP+/a-SMA+/PDRGF-b+/CD29+ = CAFs
Speaker notes: Within the live cells, I use EpCAM and CD45 markers to distinguish cancer cells (EpCAM+) and leukocytes (CD45+).

From the CD45-negative and EpCAM-negative population, which represents the non-immune stroma, I exclude endothelial cells using the CD31 marker.

Finally, from the CD31-negative population, I identify fibroblasts based on markers like FAP, α-SMA, PDGFR-β, and CD29.

This approach allows me to focus on the specific cell populations of interest while excluding irrelevant or overlapping populations.  then to the results

## Slide 17
Aim 3: Enrich CAFs from HGSC patient samples
17
Speaker notes: 3) enrich CAFs from the acquired cell suspension using magnetic-activated cell sorting (MACS)

briefly about this technique: its used to isolate specific cell populations based on their surface markers. So here, the aim is to enrich CAFs by labeling cells with magnetic beads conjugated to antibodies targeting fibroblast-specific markers. When passed through a magnetic column, CAFs are retained, while unlabeled cells flow through. The retained, labeled cells can then be eluted from the column for downstream analysis.

Tumor microenvironment (TME)
Created with BioRender.com
Speaker notes: The TME refers to a complex and dynamic network of cellular and molecular components that supports tumor growth. 
Various cell types contribute to the TME: cancer cells, immune cells, and the nonimmune stromal cells including cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) , mesothelial cells and endothelial cells. 
soluble factors like growth factors and cytokines, as well as capillaries and the extracellular matrix (ECM)

ls, immune cells, and the nonimmune stromal cells including cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) , mesothelial cells and endothelial cells. 
soluble factors like growth factors and cytokines, as well as capillaries and the extracellular matrix (ECM)
all these are essential in shaping the TME and affecting tumor dynamics

## Slide 4
4
Created with BioRender.com
Speaker notes: Among TME components, cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) have been gaining increasing recognition with their importance in affecting the clinical outcomes

## Slide 5
Cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs)
5
Created with BioRender.com
Speaker notes: CAFs are activated fibroblasts, exhibiting distinct cellular phenotypes influenced by their surrounding microenvironment 
A highly heterogenous population within the TME, and different subtypes on CAFs has shown to have different effects within the TME
but generally
A high amounts of CAFs in epithelial ovarian cancer tumors is linked to advanced-stage disease such as the presence of lymph node and omentum metastases. 
CAFs contribute to tumor growth by interacting with cancer cells and influencing the extracellular matrix remodeling. 
Then, the synthesis and remodeling of the ECM by CAFs further promote metastasis and impact angiogenesis, 
and changes in ECM composition can affect immune cell infiltration and module immune responses
And this all contributes to poor drug delivery and therapeutic response

BME or more specifically commercially available Cultrex is a BME matrix 
that is derived from mouse sarcoma cells.

when increasing the BME matrix concentration, the spheroid stability is compromised
OmGel as human derived matrix 
offers the most physiologically relevant but also requires most effort

BME = composed of laminin, collagen type IV, entactin, and heparan sulfate proteoglycans

Optimized dissociation and enrichment protocols enabled robust CAF recovery for HGSC model development.
Sequential dissociation protocol ensured comprehensive recovery of key TME cell populations.
All tested culture conditions supported CAF spheroid formation, though matrix choice remains critical for future model optimization.

 This thesis paves the way for developing a more TME-representative HGSC model, with predictive potential for therapeutic development.

Key outcomes of this thesis

Then, I evaluated the three different culture conditions. While all supported the spheroid formation and growth, the choice of matrix is aa critical factor for future model development. 
Overall, these findings lay the groundwork for establishing a TME-representative model of HGSC. This novel model has predictive potential for disease modeling and could serve as a valuable platform for testing new treatment strategies.

## Slide 23
Gating strategy for flow cytometry analysis
23
EpCAM+ = Cancer cells
CD45+ = Leukocytes
EpCAM- CD45- = Non-immune stroma
EpCAM- CD45- CD31+ = Endothelial cells
EpCAM- CD45- CD31- FAP+/a-SMA+/PDRGF-b+/CD29+ = CAFs
Speaker notes: First, I gate on cells to exclude debris, which are small particles or cell fragments not relevant to the analysis.
Next, I select single cells to ensure I’m analyzing individual cells, not doublets or aggregates.
From the single-cell population, I gate the live cells, excluding any dead cells.
Within the live cells, I use EpCAM and CD45 markers to distinguish cancer cells (EpCAM+) and leukocytes (CD45+).

From the CD45-negative and EpCAM-negative population, which represents the non-immune stroma, I exclude endothelial cells using the CD31 marker.
Finally, from the CD31-negative population, I identify fibroblasts based on markers like FAP, α-SMA, PDGFR-β, and CD29.

egative and EpCAM-negative population, which represents the non-immune stroma, I exclude endothelial cells using the CD31 marker.
Finally, from the CD31-negative population, I identify fibroblasts based on markers like FAP, α-SMA, PDGFR-β, and CD29.
This approach allows me to focus on the specific cell populations of interest while excluding irrelevant or overlapping populations.  then to the results

## Slide 4
FAP,PDGFRA, and PDGFRB in HGSC
PDGFRB and PDGFRA:
Some studies report that high stromal PDGFRB correlates with poor prognosis, but findings are inconsistent.
Based on IHC of PDGFRB and PDGFRA in stromal and tumor compartments in HGSC, PDGFRA and PDGFRB doesn’t have prognostic benefit in HGSC [1]. 
FAP:
High FAP⁺ fibroblast abundance alone was not associated with shorter survival in the whole cohort [2].
However, when both CD8⁺ T-cell density and FAP⁺ fibroblast levels were high, outcomes were significantly worse:
In the discovery cohort:
High FAP + high CD8 → shorter PFS (11.4 vs. 18.6 months, p = 0.01)
In the validation cohort:
High FAP + high CD8 → shorter OS (31.5 vs. 76.9 months, p = 0.02)

Madsen, C. V., Steffensen, K. D., Waldstrøm, M., & Jakobsen, A. (2012). Immunohistochemical expression of platelet-derived growth factor receptors in ovarian cancer patients with long-term follow-up. Pathology Research International, 2012, 851432. https://doi.org/10.1155/2012/851432
Corvigno, S., Fernebro, J., Karlsson, J.

## Slide 5
Is using spatial data suitable for our purposes?
the ROIs in the GeoMX selected based on the immune cell presence - bias in immune cell composition in the ROIs

Chromosome Segregation: Enriched (Fold Enrichment = 7.81), significant (p-value = 2.16E-06).
DNA Replication: Significant enrichment (Fold Enrichment = 8.78), (p-value = 6.28E-05).
Mitotic Regulation:
Regulation of Mitotic Cell Cycle Phase Transition: Highly enriched (Fold Enrichment = 8.94), significant (p-value = 1.61E-07).
Negative Regulation of Mitotic Cell Cycle: Significant enrichment (Fold Enrichment = 9.94), (p-value = 6.60E-06).

## Slide 10
Conclusions from Matt-Whitney comparisons of most variable genes expressions
Stroma-enriched CAFs (matrix-constructive phenotype):
ECM production: COL1A1, COL3A1, FN1, TNC, DPT, MGP
Metabolism/differentiation: ADH1B, ADIPOQ, AKR1C2, HSD11B1, FMO2
Chemokines/immune crosstalk: CCL19, CXCL1, CXCL3, CXCL14, PF4, PPBP
Functional profile: ECM construction, immune recruitment, metabolic specialization
TSI-enriched CAFs (activated, tumor-promoting phenotype):
Wnt pathway modulation: SFRP2, SFRP4
Stress/immune response: IFI6, ISG15, DUSP1, EGR1
Angiogenesis/remodeling: THBS1, TIMP3, MMP
Functional profile: Activated, stress-responsive, angiogenic niche shapers
But! Some genes related to cell adhesion and matrix deposition also upregulated COL1A1, COL3A1, FN1,
Distant stroma CAFs:
Matrix-constructive, metabolically specialized, immune-recruiting
Likely support tumor growth via ECM deposition and immune modulation
TSI CAFs:
Activated, stress-responsive, pro-angiogenic
Shape the tumor invasive front and aid therapy resistance

## Slide 1

Kürşat Birgin
06.10.2025
1

## Slide 2
Data Overview & Strategy
Single-cell RNA-seq: multi-patient dataset of tumor, fibroblast, and epithelial cells

Spatial transcriptomics (ST): fibroblast signatures from separate samples

Integration approach:

Currently; patient-independent projection of ST signatures onto scRNA-seq

Later; patient-specific mapping, requires merging ST and scRNA-seq patient tables

## Slide 3
Current Code
Load required R packages
Load scRNA-seq Seurat object (all cells, patient-independent) [from Sara]
Load spatial transcriptomics (ST) fibroblast signature gene lists (Top100, Top200) [from Nika]

Extract genes from ST data and intersect with genes present in the scRNA-seq object 
Subset scRNA-seq to Fibroblasts and Epithelial cells (focus on tumor–stroma interface) 

Note: “Epithelial cells” may include non-tumor cells; to be double-checked [Sara?]

Reduce memory by keeping only variable features + ST signature genes

Generate DotPlot showing Top100 signature genes across cell types
Dot size = fraction of cells expressing a gene; color = relative expression

## Slide 4
Here: Top 10/100 features/genes
Gene(s) | Main Function / Pathway | Interpretation in Context
SFRP2 / SFRP4 | WNT pathway modulators | Classic CAF markers, enriched at tumor–stroma interface (TSI)
MMP11 | ECM remodeling enzyme | CAF-specific, supports tumor invasion at TSI
FOS | Stress-responsive transcription factor | Marks activated fibroblasts at TSI
C7 | Complement system component | Indicates immune-modulatory fibroblasts
CCL19 | Immune cell chemoattractant | Marks fibroblasts in TLS-like immune niches
FDCSP | Follicular dendritic cell–related stromal protein | Linked to TLS or immune-interacting stroma

ement system component | Indicates immune-modulatory fibroblasts
CCL19 | Immune cell chemoattractant | Marks fibroblasts in TLS-like immune niches
FDCSP | Follicular dendritic cell–related stromal protein | Linked to TLS or immune-interacting stroma
MMP9 | ECM-degrading enzyme | Inflammation-linked, matrix-remodeling fibroblasts
CLU | Secreted chaperone, stress response | Stress/tissue-remodeling in both tumor and stroma
MGP | Matrix Gla protein (ECM deposition) | ECM‐producing, myofibroblast‐like fibroblasts enriched in stromal zones.
v
v
Note: Currently, the dataset doesn’t separate tumor vs normal epithelial cells explicitly. For now, I used all epithelial cells as a tumor proxy to visualize fibroblast–epithelial interactions at the tumor–stroma interface (TSI).

I used (thanks to Anna V.):

# Min-max rescalerescale01 <- function(x) {  (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))}

to rescale, so the lowest score becomes 0, the highest becomes 1, and all other values are proportionally scaled in between.

## Slide 8
Calculation for Normalized Enrichment Score (NES)
Raw enrichment score:

Average log2 fold change of each gene between TSI_high and Stroma_high is calculated
Genes are ranked by how much they are higher in TSI_high versus Stroma_high 
GSEA checks whether genes from each pathway tend to appear near the top or bottom of this list 
A running sum over the ranked genes gives the raw enrichment score (ES)

Random permutations: To know whether the observed clustering is unusual, GSEA shuffles the ranked list many times and recalculates the enrichment score for each shuffle. This creates a “null distribution” of scores we would expect by chance.

Normalization: The raw score is then divided by the average of these random scores (or scaled based on the null distribution) to account for differences in pathway size (big pathways naturally get higher raw scores) and the overall distribution of the ranked genes.

Also: NES direction shows which group the pathway is enriched in (Positive NES → enriched in TSI_high / Negative NES → enriched in Stroma_high), and its magnitude reflects how strongly and consistently the genes shift in that direction.

**Experimental Plan**

Experiment: Dissociation protocol optimization (\***Insert sample ID
here**\*)

Objective: Optimize the dissociation protocol to isolate more
fibroblasts from ovarian tumors.

Approaches:

1\. \*Using collagenase & hyaluronidase\*:

- Condition 1: 20 min 0.125 % trypsin and 1 hour C&H

- Condition 2: 20 min 0.125 % trypsin and 2 hours C&H

- Condition 3: 20 min 0.125 % trypsin, 1 hour C&H, followed by brief
 > 0.125% trypsin of the cell pellet

- Condition 4: 20 min 0.125 % trypsin, 2 hours C&H, followed by brief
 > 0.125% trypsin of the cell pellet

2\. \*Using dispase followed by collagenase & hyaluronidase\*:

- Condition 5: Dispase for 30 min, followed by C&H for 30 min

- Condition 6: Dispase for 30 min, followed by C&H for 1 hour

Experimental Steps:

1\. \*Sample Preparation\*:

- Collect ovarian tumor samples and wash the larger tumor pieces by
 > placing them into a 50 ml Falcon tube, pouring sterile HBSS on
 > top, and gently inverting the tube several times.

- Mince the tumor piece into small pieces using sterile scalpel and
 > scissors; very small pieces for C&H dissociation and bigger but
 > still small pieces for dispase dissociation.

- Divide the minced tissue into six equal parts for each condition.

 > 10 ml 0.25% trypsin EDTA

trypsin EDTA

4. Vortex the bead vials and add one drop of both positive (BD
 > CompBeads AntiMouse IgG, #51-9006274) and negative (CompBeads
 > Negative Control #51-9006227) compensation beads to each tube.

 a. Note! Ensure that the drops have reached the bottom of the tube.

5. For stained compensation controls, add 2 µL of each antibody.

 a. Except for a-SMA, for which 2.5 µL should be added.

 b. DCM cannot be used for compensation controls, so it should be
 > replaced with an antibody labeled with the same fluorophore
 > (BV510). For example, BV510 CD16 (#563829, BD Biosciences).

6. Incubate for at least 30 min at +4°C shielded from light.

7. Resuspend the beads in 350 µL FACS buffer.

8. Keep the compensations on ice and protected from light until running
 > on Fortessa.

9. Before running the sample, these compensations should be used to
 > determine the PMT voltages for each fluorochrome.

**Protocol for organoid imaging and charting with ImageJ & Excel**

1. On the final day of culturing (before fixation), take pictures of
 > 3-4 wells of each condition with the EVOS microscope (10x
 > magnification). It is good to take several pictures in order to
 > make sure that we get pictures of sufficient quality for
 > analyzing.

> Label each picture with all necessary information, ex.
> S034_pAdn_ome_p0_d7_10x.

2. Import the images to your computer and open them in ImageJ/Fiji. Set
 > the scale in ImageJ according to the 10x magnification of the
 > images as follows:

> ![Kuva, joka sisältää kohteen näyttökuva Kuvaus luotu
> automaattisesti](media/image2.png){width="2.209086832895888in"
> height="2.2252701224846896in"}
>
> (This scaling was determined according to information found on
> http://microscopynotes.com/evos/.)

a. Analyze -\> Set scale

b. Set scale (known distance) based on this table

c. ![](media/image1.png){width="2.7682819335083115in"
 > height="1.8543088363954505in"}

```{=html}

```
3. Analyze each clear organoid from each image/field using the
 > oval/elliptical/freehand selections tools. Choose the tool based
 > on the organoid shape and define each organoid as accurately as
 > possible. When you have circled the whole organoid, go to Analyze
 > 🡪 Tools 🡪 ROI manager and choose Add \[t\]. This will add the
 > organoid to a list.

4. Select the organoid you're working with from the list and rename it.
 > Use numbers starting from 1 for each image/field, this allows you
 > to easily determine the sum of organoids as well.

> (By using the ROI manager, you can later go back to the images and
> determine which organoid corresponds to which values on the organoid
> excel chart.)

**Experimental Plan**

Experiment: Dissociation protocol optimization

Objective: Optimize the dissociation protocol to isolate more
fibroblasts from ovarian tumors.

Approaches:

1\. \*Using collagenase & hyaluronidase\*:

- Collagenase & Hyaluronidase (C&H) (10x stock, #07912, STEMCELL
 > Technologies, stored in the wet lab freezer Enzymes box) for 1
 > hour

2\. \*Using dispase followed by collagenase & hyaluronidase\*:

- Dispase (50 U/ml stock, #D4693-1G, Sigma Merck, stored in the wet
 > lab fridge) for 30 min followed by C&H for 1 hour.

- This approach includes dispase-only condition.

Media used in this protocol is DMEM/F12+++, which is DMEM/F12
(#11320033, Gibco) supplemented with 1x GlutaMAX (#35050061, Gibco), 10
nM HEPES (#H0887, Sigma-Aldrich) and 1% penstrep
(Penicillin-Streptomycin, 10,000 U/mL, #15140122, Gibco). Media is
premade in the cell culture room fridge.

Experimental Steps:

1\. \*Sample Preparation\*:

- Collect ovarian tumor samples and wash larger tumor pieces by
 > placing them into a 50 ml Falcon tube, pouring sterile HBSS
 > (#14175-053, Gibco) on top, and gently inverting the tube several
 > times.

- Mince the tumor piece into small pieces (using a sterile scalpel and
 > scissors on a 10 cm cell culture plate.

- Divide the minced tissue into 2 equal parts.

2\. \*Dissociation Protocols\*:

\*Dispase followed by collagenase condition\*:

- Treat the first half of the minced sample with dispase for 30
 > minutes at +37°C rotating. Vortex (approximately 15 seconds)
 > before, in the middle, and after incubation.

 - Prepare dispase dissociation media: 9.7 ml DMEM/F12+++ + 200 µl
 > dispase (50 U/ml stock) + 100 µl RQ1 RNase-Free DNase (#M6101,
 > Promega, stored wet lab freezer Enzymes box), and place the
 > tissue pieces in.

middle, and after incubation.

 - Prepare dispase dissociation media: 9.7 ml DMEM/F12+++ + 200 µl
 > dispase (50 U/ml stock) + 100 µl RQ1 RNase-Free DNase (#M6101,
 > Promega, stored wet lab freezer Enzymes box), and place the
 > tissue pieces in.

- Filter the samples with a 70 µm cell strainer (#22-363-548, Fisher)
 > to obtain single-cell suspensions.

 - Pre-wet the filter with 1 ml DMEM/F12+++, transfer the digested
 > suspension through the filter into a 50 ml Falcon tube, wash
 > the stainer with 5 ml media, and **keep the tissue
 > leftovers.**

- **For obtained cell suspension (dispase only):**

 - Centrifuge the cell suspension for 10 min 1000 rpm at +4°C

 - Do the Red Blood Cell (RBC) lysis (#11814389001, Sigma-Aldrich):
 > add 2-4 ml depending on the pellet size, mix thoroughly,
 > incubate 5 min RT, and add 10-20 ml cold DMEM/F12+++ media.

 - Centrifuge the cell suspension for 10 min 1000 rpm at +4°C, and
 > resuspend to DMEM/F12+++.

 - Count the cells and prepare the aliquots for flow
 > cytometry/biobanking.

- **Further, dissociate the leftover tissue pieces with C&H** for 1 h
 > at +37°C rotating. Vortex before and after the incubation period.

 - Prepare C&H dissociation media: 8.9 ml DMEM/F12+++ media + 1 ml
 > C&H (10x stock) + 100 µl RQ1 RNase-Free DNase, and place the
 > tissue pieces in.

- Filter the samples with a 70 µm cell strainer to obtain single-cell
 > suspensions

 - Pre-wet the filter with 1 ml DMEM/F12+++, transfer the digested
 > suspension through the filter into a 50 ml Falcon tube, wash
 > the stainer with 5 ml media, and discard the tissue leftovers.

 - Centrifuge the cell suspension for 10 min 1000 rpm at +4°C

- Do the RBC lysis: add 2-4 ml depending on the pellet size, mix

sion through the filter into a 50 ml Falcon tube, wash
 > the stainer with 5 ml media, and discard the tissue leftovers.

 - Centrifuge the cell suspension for 10 min 1000 rpm at +4°C

- Do the RBC lysis: add 2-4 ml depending on the pellet size, mix
 > thoroughly, incubate 5 min RT, and add 10-20 ml cold DMEM/F12+++
 > media.

- Centrifuge the cell suspension for 10 min 1000 rpm at +4°C, and
 > resuspend to DMEM/F12+++.

- Count the cells and prepare the aliquots for flow cytometry.

\*Collagenase & hyaluronidase-only condition\*:

- Treat the second half of the tissue pieces with C&H for 1 hour at
 > +37°C rotating. Vortex (approximately 15 seconds) before, in the
 > middle, and after incubation.

 - Prepare C&H dissociation media: 8.9 ml DMEM/F12+++ media + 1 ml
 > C&H (10x stock) + 100 µl RQ1 RNase-Free DNase, and place the
 > tissue pieces in.

- Filter the samples with a 70 µm cell strainer to obtain single-cell
 > suspensions

 - Pre-wet the filter with 1 ml DMEM/F12+++, transfer the digested
 > suspension through the filter into a 50 ml Falcon tube, wash
 > the stainer with 5 ml media, and discard the tissue leftovers.

 - Centrifuge the cell suspension for 10 min 1000 rpm at +4°C

- Do the RBC lysis: add 2-4 ml depending on the pellet size, mix
 > thoroughly, incubate 5 min RT, and add 10-20 ml cold DMEM/F12+++
 > media.

- Centrifuge the cell suspension for 10 min 1000 rpm at +4°C, and
 > resuspend to DMEM/F12+++.

- Count the cells and prepare the aliquots for flow cytometry.

3\. \*Flow Cytometry\*:

Prepare aliquots from each dissociation method (3) for multicolor
staining, and from the Dispase + C&H dissociated cells, prepare FMOs (8)
and an unstained control. Each aliquot should contain up to 1.5 × 10⁶
cells.

Calculations for multicolor samples:

-+-------------+

- Wash cells once by adding 900 µl FACS buffer (2 % FBS, 2 mM EDTA in
 > PBS, stored in the wet lab fridge) and centrifuge 6 min 400 g at
 > +4°C (you may increase to 450 g if a pellet is not forming).

- Surface antibody staining

 - For multicolor samples, and a-SMA and PDGFRb FMO samples:
 > Calculate the required amounts of antibodies based on the
 > table above and prepare an antibody mix by diluting them in
 > Brilliant Stain Buffer (#563794, BD Biosciences, stored in the
 > wet lab fridge). Add 50 µl of the antibody mix to each
 > multicolor sample.

 - For FMO samples: First, add the required amount of Brilliant
 > Stain Buffer to each sample, as indicated in the table above.
 > Then, add each antibody one at a time, excluding the antibody
 > corresponding to the fluorophore being tested in the FMO
 > sample.

 - Incubate for 30 min at RT shielded from light.

- After incubation, wash once with 900 µl FACS buffer (centrifuge 6
 > min 400 g at +4°C).

- Fix cells by adding 200 µl IC Fixation buffer (#00-8222, Invitrogen,
 > stored in the wet lab fridge) and incubate for 40 min at +4°C
 > shielded from light.

- After incubation, add 1 ml 1x Permeabilization buffer (#00-8333-56,
 > Invitrogen, diluted in mQ-water, stored in the wet lab fridge),
 > and incubate 5 min at +4°C.

- Centrifuge 6 min 400 g at +4°C, wash pellet with 1 ml 1x
 > Permeabilization buffer, and resuspend in 900 µl FACS buffer for
 > overnight storage.

- Next day, start by centrifuging 6 min 400 g at +4°C.

- Intracellular antibody staining

 - For multicolor samples and FMOs samples excluding a-SMA and
 > PDGFRb: Calculate the required amounts of antibodies based on
 > the table above and prepare an antibody mix by diluting them
 > in 1x Permeabilization buffer.

lular antibody staining

 - For multicolor samples and FMOs samples excluding a-SMA and
 > PDGFRb: Calculate the required amounts of antibodies based on
 > the table above and prepare an antibody mix by diluting them
 > in 1x Permeabilization buffer. Add 50 µl of the antibody mix
 > to each sample.

 - For a-SMA and PDGFRb FMO samples: First, add the required amount

 > the table above. Then, add the remaining antibody to the
 > cells: add a-SMA antibody to the PDGFRb FMO sample and PDGFRb
 > antibody to the a-SMA FMO sample.

 - Incubate for 30 min at RT shielded from light.

- Wash once with 1 ml 1x Permeabilization buffer (centrifuge 6 min 400
 > g at +4°C) and resuspend in 400 µl FACS buffer.

- Pipeted the cell suspension through a 5 ml Falcon polystyrene tube
 > with a cell-strainer cap (blue) (#352235 Corning). Avoid bubbles.

- Keep the samples on ice and protected from light until running at
 > Fortessa.

Prepare compensation controls

- Use Falcon 5 ml polystyrene round-bottom tubes (white cap) (#352052
 > Corning).

- Prepare one unstained and for each fluorophore one compensation
 > tube.

- Use BD CompBeads Plus (7.5 µm) compensation beads (#560497, BD
 > bioscience, stored in the wet lab fridge), first vortex gently and
 > then add one drop of both positive (BD CompBeads AntiMouse IgG,
 > #51-9006274) and negative (CompBeads Negative Control #51-9006227)
 > compensation beads to each tube.

- For stained compensation controls, add 2 µl of each antibody, except
 > for a-SMA, for which 2.5 µl should be added. DCM cannot be used
 > for compensation controls, so it should be replaced with an
 > antibody labeled with the same fluorophore (BV510).

- Incubate for at least 30 min at +4°C shielded from light, and
